writing_task: Hey, I need help with some copy for an empty state screen. We have a dashboard section called "Scheduled Payments" — it shows upcoming automatic payments a user has set up. When someone lands here for the first time and hasn't scheduled anything yet, we just have a blank section with no copy at all right now. I need a headline and 2–3 lines of supporting copy (plus a button label) that nudges them toward setting up their first scheduled payment without feeling like a sales pitch.

drafts.rubric:
No scheduled payments yet

Set one up and your recurring payments — bills, subscriptions, transfers — run on the dates you choose, without having to come back and trigger them manually. You stay in control of the schedule; the payments just happen.

Schedule a payment
drafts.generic:
Nothing scheduled yet

Set up a payment once and let it run on its own schedule — no reminders needed, no due dates to chase. Whether it's a recurring bill or a regular transfer, scheduled payments handle the timing for you.

You stay in control and can adjust or cancel anytime.

Schedule a Payment
drafts.preference:
No scheduled payments yet

Set up a scheduled payment and it'll show up here — so you always know what's coming and when.

Schedule a Payment

user_reason: Draft A wins because it does exactly what an empty state should do — tells the user what's missing, explains the value in one clean sentence, and points them toward the action. The button label 'Schedule a Payment' describes the outcome. It's not trying to be clever. Draft B has the right instincts but runs long — 'You stay in control of the schedule; the payments just happen' feels like it wandered in from a marketing one-pager. The semicolon construction especially reads like copy that's trying too hard. Draft C loses me at 'no reminders needed, no due dates to chase' — that's feature marketing dressed up as UI copy, and the second paragraph feels tacked on to justify the word count. 'Nothing scheduled yet' is also slightly weaker than 'No scheduled payments yet' because it's less specific about what the section actually does. A is the only one I'd actually ship without edits.

criteria_analysis.name: Functional completeness
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft names the empty state, explains what scheduled payments do (bills, subscriptions, transfers), specifies user control over dates, and points to a clear CTA — fully standalone. The generic draft also covers what scheduled payments do and includes a CTA but adds an 'adjust or cancel anytime' line that drifts slightly from the core job. The original preference draft is functional but thin — it tells users things will 'show up here' without giving enough context about what scheduled payments actually do or why they'd want one.

criteria_analysis.name: User-centered direction
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft puts the user in motion throughout — 'you choose,' 'you stay in control' — and the CTA describes the action precisely. The generic draft leans product-centered: 'scheduled payments handle the timing for you' makes the product the agent, and 'let it run on its own schedule' frames the product doing work rather than the user acting. The original preference draft keeps the user as subject ('Set up… and it'll show up') with good forward momentum, though it's lighter on specificity.

criteria_analysis.name: Trust through specificity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft earns trust by naming concrete categories (bills, subscriptions, transfers) and specifying what the user controls (the dates, the schedule) without overclaiming. The generic draft uses vague reassurance ('no reminders needed, no due dates to chase') that leans toward cheerleading rather than specificity. The original preference draft offers almost no concrete detail — 'what's coming and when' is abstract rather than specific.

criteria_analysis.name: Economy and precision
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The original preference draft is the leanest — every word does work, nothing redundant, instantly scannable. The rubric-guided draft is tight but 'without having to come back and trigger them manually' is slightly wordy and could be trimmed. The generic draft has clear throat-clearing ('no reminders needed, no due dates to chase') and the 'adjust or cancel anytime' line feels like filler that doesn't serve the empty-state moment.

criteria_analysis.name: Tonal calibration
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft hits the calm, grounded register perfectly — matter-of-fact without being clinical, warm without selling. The generic draft tips into breezy sales-pitch territory: 'no reminders needed, no due dates to chase' reads like marketing copy, and 'handle the timing for you' has a customer-service voice. The original preference draft is calm and grounded but almost too quiet — it works tonally but doesn't quite achieve the 'competent colleague' presence.

criteria_analysis.name: Contextual presence
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft feels written for this specific screen — someone seeing an empty scheduled payments section for the first time. The original preference draft also feels moment-specific with 'it'll show up here' anchoring to the screen. The generic draft reads like it could be on a marketing page or feature tour — 'Whether it's a recurring bill or a regular transfer' is feature-selling copy, not empty-state copy written for where the user actually is.

The generic draft also covers what scheduled payments do and includes a CTA but adds an 'adjust or cancel anytime' line that drifts slightly from the core job. The original preference draft is functional but thin — it tells users things will 'show up here' without giving enough context about what scheduled payments actually do or why they'd want one.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft best balances this user's priorities: functionally complete, user-centered, specific, tonally grounded, and written for the moment. The original preference draft is admirably economical and tonally right but too thin on context and specificity to stand fully on its own. The generic draft consistently drifts into marketing-speak and product-as-agent framing that this user would reject.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both are clear, but Draft B more explicitly explains the benefit (no reminders needed, no due dates to chase) and separates the control message into its own line, making it easier to parse quickly.
Draft B flows more naturally from problem to solution to reassurance. Draft A's middle sentence uses an em dash list that slightly disrupts the logical flow.
Both drafts are grammatically correct with proper punctuation and no errors.

Draft B separates the reassurance ('You stay in control and can adjust or cancel anytime') into its own paragraph, creating a cleaner three-part structure. Draft A compresses everything into one body paragraph.
Both are reasonably engaging for UI copy. Draft B's benefit-focused language (no reminders, no due dates to chase) is slightly more compelling, but Draft A's directness is also effective.
Draft B strikes a more conversational and reassuring tone that feels polished and user-friendly. Draft A is solid but slightly more utilitarian in its phrasing.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft B is better organized with a clearer structure, more user-centric benefit language, and a reassuring closing line that addresses a common concern (ability to adjust or cancel). Both are well-written, but B feels more polished and thoughtfully composed.

suggestion_text: - **Economy and precision**: The original preference draft scored highest here (5 vs. 4 for rubric-guided), and the user ranked it first overall — specifically calling out the rubric-guided draft's semicolon construction as 'copy that's trying too hard.' The criterion itself is sound, but the description needs to explicitly flag that compound constructions and over-explanation of mechanics are failure modes, so future rubric-guided drafts don't trade brevity for thoroughness.

suggestion_reasons.Economy and precision: The original preference draft scored highest here (5 vs. 4 for rubric-guided), and the user ranked it first overall — specifically calling out the rubric-guided draft's semicolon construction as 'copy that's trying too hard.' The criterion itself is sound, but the description needs to explicitly flag that compound constructions and over-explanation of mechanics are failure modes, so future rubric-guided drafts don't trade brevity for thoroughness.

suggested_rubric.name: Functional completeness
suggested_rubric.description: Every standalone piece of copy does its full job without depending on another element to close the loop. Messages name the problem, provide relevant context, and point to the next action — all within the same unit. A user who sees only that one element should have enough to act.

dimensions.label: Clearly states what happened or went wrong
Gives enough 'why' for the user to understand the situation without guessing
Includes a clear next step or direction within the same element
Element makes sense and feels complete if the user never sees the next screen

suggested_rubric.name: User-centered direction
suggested_rubric.description: The user is the subject of the copy, not the product or the situation. Copy puts the user in motion — they are the one acting, finding, fixing — rather than watching the product assess or narrate. CTAs tell the user exactly what will happen when they click. Second person, present tense by default.

dimensions.label: The user is positioned as the agent doing the action, not the product
Buttons and links describe the outcome of clicking, not vague verbs like 'try again' or 'submit'
Nothing in the copy implies the user made a mistake or did something wrong
Copy orients toward the next action rather than dwelling on or explaining the current state

suggested_rubric.name: Trust through specificity
suggested_rubric.description: Reassurance is earned by naming concrete realities the user cares about — saved progress, nothing charged, specific causes — not by using reassurance language ('don't worry,' 'no problem'). Confidence comes from being specific, not from telling the user to feel confident. No hedging and no overclaiming.

dimensions.label: Reassuring statements reference specific, verifiable facts rather than emotional soothing
Avoids 'probably,' 'likely,' 'might,' or other language that undercuts the copy's own confidence
Does not promise outcomes the product cannot guarantee
Proactively resolves concerns the user would have but might not articulate (e.g., 'was I charged?' 'did I lose my cart?')

suggested_rubric.name: Economy and precision
suggested_rubric.description: Every word does specific, identifiable work. No throat-clearing, no filler, no over-introducing self-explanatory elements. Copy is short and scannable. If a list has clear labels and descriptions, the line above it doesn't need to explain what the list is. Completeness never justifies length — a draft that explains its own mechanics or uses compound constructions to add thoroughness has failed this criterion even if every sentence is technically accurate.

dimensions.label: No setup lines, preambles, or transition phrases that delay the useful content
Removing any word would change or lose the meaning
Structure supports quick visual parsing — short sentences, clear hierarchy, labeled sections
Does not introduce or describe elements that are already self-explanatory from their content
Does not explain how a feature works when naming what it does is sufficient — compound constructions and mechanism descriptions are treated as word count failures

suggested_rubric.name: Tonal calibration
suggested_rubric.description: Copy hits a precise register: calm and grounded without being clinical, warm without being breezy, confident without cheerleading. The target is 'the copy equivalent of a calm nod' — the way a competent colleague would talk to you after a snag. No pep talks, no customer-service voice, no sardonic edge, no shrugging.

dimensions.label: Avoids pep-talk language, forced optimism, or performative warmth
Does not read like a system log, support article, or procedure document
Reads as grounded and steady rather than casual or dismissive
Never anticipates negative user behavior or introduces anxiety that wasn't already there
Sounds like something a real person would actually say — plain, direct, conversational without performing conversational

suggested_rubric.name: Contextual presence
suggested_rubric.description: Copy feels written for the specific screen, moment, and emotional state the user is in — not lifted from a generic pattern library. It should be clear from the copy alone what just happened, without the copy explicitly narrating or commenting on the situation. The line should feel like it knows where the user is.

dimensions.label: Copy would feel out of place on a different screen or in a different context
Addresses the question the user is silently asking, not an adjacent or assumed question
Acknowledges the situation without describing or commenting on it from the outside
Does not comment on the difficulty, commonality, or expected duration of the situation

writing_task: Hey, I need help with copy for an empty state screen. It's for the **Payees** section of our small business banking app — this is what users see the first time they log in, before they've added anyone to pay. The audience is small business owners, so they're busy and task-focused, not here to browse. I need the screen headline, a one-line supporting description, and a primary CTA button label.

drafts.rubric:
No payees yet

Add the people and businesses you pay regularly — vendors, contractors, anyone you send money to. They'll be here when you need them.

Add a Payee
drafts.generic:
Headline: Pay the right people, fast.

Supporting description: Add vendors, contractors, or anyone you pay regularly so you can send money in seconds — no re-entering details every time.

Button: Add Your First Payee
drafts.preference:
Headline: No payees yet

Description: Add the people and businesses you pay to send money fast.

Button: Add a payee

user_reason: Draft A is the strongest. The headline is honest and descriptive, the supporting copy explains what a payee is and why it matters (vendors, contractors, people you send money to) without being fluffy, and 'Add a Payee' is clean outcome-oriented copy. It also treats the empty state as a helpful nudge — it tells the user exactly what they're building and why it's useful. Draft B is acceptable but the description feels truncated and slightly awkward ('Add the people and businesses you pay to send money fast' reads like a run-on). It's functional but not polished. Draft C is the weakest for my purposes — 'Pay the right people, fast.' is a marketing headline, not a UI headline. It's clever in a way that sacrifices directness, and 'Add Your First Payee' with the capital Y feels like it's performing enthusiasm. The empty state copy should guide, not sell. C would be fine on a landing page; it doesn't belong inside the product.

criteria_analysis.name: Economy and precision
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The original preference draft is the leanest — every word earns its place and nothing is redundant. The rubric-guided draft adds useful context ('They'll be here when you need them') but the enumeration 'vendors, contractors, anyone you send money to' feels like a catalogue that restates what 'people and businesses you pay regularly' already covers. The generic draft is the wordiest — 'so you can send money in seconds — no re-entering details every time' is a benefit catalogue and the headline 'Pay the right people, fast' is a tagline, not an economical statement.

criteria_analysis.name: User-centered direction
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Both the rubric-guided and original preference drafts use 'Add a Payee' as the CTA, which names the concrete outcome of clicking. The generic draft's 'Add Your First Payee' is close but 'Your First' adds a slight performative warmth. The generic draft's headline ('Pay the right people, fast') puts the product pitch as subject rather than the user's situation. The rubric-guided draft puts the user in motion with forward-looking language. The original preference draft is compact but still user-directed.

criteria_analysis.name: Functional completeness
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft does the fullest job: names the state (no payees yet), explains what payees are and why you'd add them, and provides a clear CTA. The generic draft covers similar ground but the headline doesn't name the current state — it pitches a benefit. The original preference draft names the state and points to action but the description ('Add the people and businesses you pay to send money fast') is thin on context — a user unfamiliar with the payees concept gets minimal help understanding why this matters.

criteria_analysis.name: Trust through specificity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft earns trust by being specific about who payees are ('vendors, contractors, anyone you send money to') and what happens ('They'll be here when you need them' — concrete, verifiable). The generic draft's 'send money in seconds' overclaims slightly. The original preference draft is neutral — it doesn't overclaim but also doesn't offer specificity that builds confidence.

criteria_analysis.name: Tonal calibration
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft has a human beat — 'They'll be here when you need them' sounds like something a person would say, calm and grounded. The original preference draft is plain and steady, tonally consistent if a bit flat. The generic draft cheerleads — 'Pay the right people, fast' is a tagline, and 'in seconds' is pep-talk energy that this user explicitly rejects.

criteria_analysis.name: Contextual presence
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft feels written for this exact screen — the headline names the empty state, the body orients a first-time user, and all elements share the same voice. The original preference draft is also moment-specific but the thinness of the body means it doesn't fully answer the silent question ('what is this for?'). The generic draft reads like it was pulled from a pattern library — the headline could sit on a marketing page, and there's a tonal gap between the tagline headline and the instructional body.

per_criterion.reasoning: The original preference draft is the leanest — every word earns its place and nothing is redundant. The rubric-guided draft adds useful context ('They'll be here when you need them') but the enumeration 'vendors, contractors, anyone you send money to' feels like a catalogue that restates what 'people and businesses you pay regularly' already covers. The generic draft is the wordiest — 'so you can send money in seconds — no re-entering details every time' is a benefit catalogue and the headline 'Pay the right people, fast' is a tagline, not an economical statement.

Both the rubric-guided and original preference drafts use 'Add a Payee' as the CTA, which names the concrete outcome of clicking. The generic draft's 'Add Your First Payee' is close but 'Your First' adds a slight performative warmth. The generic draft's headline ('Pay the right people, fast') puts the product pitch as subject rather than the user's situation. The rubric-guided draft puts the user in motion with forward-looking language. The original preference draft is compact but still user-directed.

The generic draft covers similar ground but the headline doesn't name the current state — it pitches a benefit. The original preference draft names the state and points to action but the description ('Add the people and businesses you pay to send money fast') is thin on context — a user unfamiliar with the payees concept gets minimal help understanding why this matters.

The original preference draft is also moment-specific but the thinness of the body means it doesn't fully answer the silent question ('what is this for?'). The generic draft reads like it was pulled from a pattern library — the headline could sit on a marketing page, and there's a tonal gap between the tagline headline and the instructional body.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft best balances economy, functional completeness, and tonal humanity — it reads like one cohesive piece of writing for this specific moment. The original preference draft is admirably lean and tonally clean but sacrifices enough context that a first-time user might not fully understand the value of adding payees. The generic draft introduces cheerleading, overclaiming, and a tagline headline that conflict with nearly every principle this user has demonstrated across the conversation.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is immediately clear about the current state ('No payees yet') and what to do next. Draft B is also clear but its headline ('Pay the right people, fast') is slightly more abstract and less directly informative about the empty state.

Draft B flows more tightly from benefit-oriented headline to supporting detail to action, creating a cohesive persuasive arc. Draft A flows logically but the supporting text slightly over-explains with the list of examples.
Both drafts are grammatically correct with no errors or typos.

Both follow a clear headline-description-CTA structure. Draft B is slightly more polished in its organization, with a concise description that integrates examples and benefits into a single sentence rather than two.

Draft B is noticeably more engaging — the headline is action-oriented and benefit-driven, and the description emphasizes speed and convenience. Draft A is functional but reads more like a status message than a motivating prompt.

Draft B strikes a more confident, energetic tone that motivates action while remaining professional. Draft A is pleasant and conversational but slightly passive in tone ('They'll be here when you need them').

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft B is more engaging, action-oriented, and persuasive while remaining clear and well-structured. It better motivates the user to take action by leading with benefits, whereas Draft A, while perfectly clear, reads more like a neutral status description.

writing_task: Hey, I need help writing the empty state copy for a "Scheduled Payments" screen. This is for small business owners who've just set up their account but haven't scheduled anything yet. The screen will have an illustration, a headline, a short subtext line, and a CTA button — so four distinct copy elements. Want to keep it practical and nudge them toward scheduling their first payment without making it feel like a chore.

drafts.rubric:
Illustration: A clean calendar with a few dotted payment lines

Headline: No payments scheduled yet

Subtext: Set up a payment and it'll show up here — along with the status of anything you've sent.

CTA button: Schedule a Payment
drafts.generic:
Headline: Your payment schedule starts here.

Subtext: Stay on top of vendor payments, subscriptions, and recurring bills — all in one place. Set it once and let it run.

CTA Button: Schedule a Payment

---

Illustration note: A clean, simple graphic of a calendar with a checkmark or clock icon works well here — something that reads as organized and in control, not overwhelming.
drafts.preference:
Headline: No payments scheduled yet

Subtext: Set up recurring or one-time payments and we'll handle the reminders.

CTA button: Schedule a Payment

user_reason: B wins because it does something the others don't — it tells the user what they'll see once they act. 'It'll show up here — along with the status of anything you've sent' makes the empty state feel like a preview of something useful, not just a dead end. That's the nudge I want. It earns its place. A is clean and honest, but the subtext is a bit feature-listy ('recurring or one-time') and 'we'll handle the reminders' drifts toward marketing copy — I don't want promises in a UI string, I want orientation. C is the weakest. 'Your payment schedule starts here' is trying too hard to be a moment, and the subtext is doing way too much — vendor payments, subscriptions, recurring bills, 'set it and forget it' energy. It reads like onboarding marketing that got lost and ended up in the product. The CTA is identical across all three, which is fine but also a missed opportunity — 'Schedule a Payment' describes the action, not the outcome. I'd push back on that in review regardless of which draft we go with.

criteria_analysis.name: Functional completeness
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels: its headline acknowledges the empty state, the body nudges toward action AND tells the user what they'll gain (status visibility), and the CTA names the outcome — each element does distinct work that escalates into a coherent unit. The generic draft reads more like a marketing pitch than a functional empty state nudge — 'Stay on top of vendor payments, subscriptions, and recurring bills' is a feature catalog, not a guide toward a first action. The original preference draft is solid but its body ('we'll handle the reminders') is a benefit claim that doesn't orient the user to what they'll see on this screen once they act.

criteria_analysis.name: User-centered direction
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft puts the user in motion ('Set up a payment and it'll show up here') with the user as agent, the CTA names the literal outcome, and benefits are framed as what you gain (status visibility). The generic draft's 'Set it once and let it run' makes the product the subject doing work; 'Stay on top of' is slightly toward-framed but abstract. The original preference draft is user-centered but 'we'll handle the reminders' shifts agency to the product.

criteria_analysis.name: Tonal calibration
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft reads like a competent colleague — calm, plain, present, with no cheerleading or marketing register. The generic draft uses clear marketing register inside the product ('Stay on top of vendor payments, subscriptions, and recurring bills — all in one place. Set it once and let it run') which is promotional value-prop language, exactly what this user flags as a non-negotiable violation. The original preference draft is well-calibrated tonally but slightly functional/flat — it lacks the one human beat the rubric-guided draft gets with 'and it'll show up here.'

criteria_analysis.name: Contextual presence
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft clearly knows what screen it's on — 'it'll show up here' and 'the status of anything you've sent' are specific to this exact screen and answer the silent question ('what will this page do for me once I use it?'). The generic draft could be lifted from a landing page or onboarding flow — it doesn't feel written for this specific empty state moment. The original preference draft is contextually appropriate but more generic in what it promises ('we'll handle the reminders' could appear on multiple screens). The rubric-guided draft also has the strongest human beat — 'and it'll show up here' is offhand and plain.

criteria_analysis.name: Economy and precision
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The original preference draft is the tightest — every word earns its place, no filler, highly scannable. The rubric-guided draft is nearly as lean but the clause 'along with the status of anything you've sent' adds useful information at the cost of slightly more length — justified by the functional completeness it provides. The generic draft has clear throat-clearing ('Your payment schedule starts here') and a catalog of use cases ('vendor payments, subscriptions, and recurring bills') that violates the payoff-over-catalog principle.

The original preference draft is clean and well-calibrated but trades functional completeness and contextual specificity for brevity. The generic draft reads like marketing copy inside a product UI, which is a fundamental violation of this user's stated priorities.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A immediately communicates the empty state — 'No payments scheduled yet' is instantly understood. Draft B's 'Your payment schedule starts here' is clear but slightly more abstract, requiring the reader to infer that nothing has been set up yet.

Draft A flows logically: empty state headline → explanation of what will appear → action to take. Draft B also flows well but pivots from an aspirational headline to listing use cases (vendors, subscriptions, bills) that slightly broadens the scope beyond the immediate context.
Both drafts are grammatically correct with no errors or typos. Punctuation and sentence construction are clean in each.

Both follow a standard empty-state pattern (headline, subtext, CTA). Draft A includes a concise illustration note inline, while Draft B's illustration note is appended separately and is more verbose, slightly disrupting the tight structure.
Draft B is more motivating and forward-looking — 'Set it once and let it run' creates a compelling value proposition. Draft A is effective but more utilitarian and less inspiring.

Draft A strikes a perfect conversational, helpful tone for an empty state — friendly without overselling. Draft B leans slightly more toward marketing copy ('all in one place'), which can feel mildly out of place in a product UI empty state.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger empty-state UI copy: it clearly communicates the current state, sets expectations for what will appear, and uses a warm, conversational tone perfectly suited to the context. Draft B is more engaging and aspirational but reads slightly more like marketing copy than in-product guidance.
